code_verification: receiver method exists on the receiver type
static methods are called statically and member methods are called on valid instances
assignment target is compatible with the method return type
argument count, argument type, and overload shape match the public API
enum owner and enum value both exist
property constants use the correct owner and value type
signal callback signatures match the public signal typedef
code blocks do not rely on inferred namespaces or overloads from naming alone
draft_requirements: Write enough practical detail for application developers to use the feature in a dali-ui app.
Ground natural-language behavior claims in the actual public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when the context pack is not enough.
Before writing examples, inspect the referenced public headers and relevant sample files when needed.
Prefer examples copied or adapted from existing samples when they match the feature; otherwise keep examples small and verify every API use.
After drafting, review the completed Markdown line by line against repository evidence and fix inaccurate explanations or code before returning.
repair_requirements: Repair only the problematic spans and the smallest surrounding context needed; do not rewrite the whole document.
For each listed issue, use the repository evidence and context pack to verify the corrected wording or code before returning.
If an issue reveals the same API misuse nearby, fix the nearby equivalent misuse too; do not make unrelated style rewrites.
After repair, re-check the edited lines against public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when needed.

rules: Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.
Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.
Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples.

usage_patterns.intent: Create and configure the primary object using public dali-ui APIs.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Prefer typed constructors and setters when available.

usage_patterns.intent: React to user or resource events through public signals.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Use signal connection APIs only with their owning trait or class.

rendered_prompt:
You are reviewing an existing DALi dali-ui application developer guide draft.
This is Phase 2.3.5: source-grounded prose review.

Primary goal:
- Improve natural-language accuracy and guide flow using repository evidence.
- Preserve the document's useful structure and examples whenever possible.
- Do not perform a full rewrite.
- Keep the revised user-facing guide in en only.
- Remove Korean, Japanese, Chinese, or other non-English prose from the revised guide.

Feature target:
- Feature: Interactive View (interactive-view)
- Category: views-components
- Guide profile: dali-ui-app
- Audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

Review rules:
- Review every natural-language paragraph line by line against public headers, implementation files, samples, and UTCs when needed.
- Accuracy is the first priority. Flow is the second priority.
- Edit the smallest unit needed: word, sentence, paragraph, or section-local addition/removal.
- If a word-level change is enough, change only the word.
- If a sentence-level change is enough, change only the sentence.
- If an entire paragraph is based on a wrong premise, revise, add, or remove that paragraph.
- Preserve code blocks when possible.
- If prose can be corrected to match an already accurate code block, prefer changing prose.
- If the code block itself makes the prose inaccurate or breaks the guide flow, rewrite only the smallest necessary part of that code block and verify it against public headers/source.
- Do not invent APIs.
- Do not add generic filler just to make the document longer.
- Do not change section structure unless prose_review.allow_section_restructure is true or the current prose is clearly misleading.
- Prefer localized edits when prose_review.localized_edits_only is true.
- Preserve code blocks especially when prose_review.preserve_code_blocks is true.
- Prefer Dali::Ui::View, typed setters, fluent chaining, and view-tree idioms for app-facing guidance.
- Treat dali-core and dali-adaptor APIs as reference/context unless the feature explicitly requires them.

Output contract:
- Put the revised full Markdown between -----BEGIN_REVISED_MARKDOWN----- and -----END_REVISED_MARKDOWN-----.
- Put the concise prose review report between -----BEGIN_PROSE_REVIEW_REPORT----- and -----END_PROSE_REVIEW_REPORT-----.
- Do not include commentary outside those markers.
- Do not wrap the revised Markdown in a code fence.
- Inside the Markdown markers, output the complete final Markdown document only.
- If no prose changes are needed, put the full unchanged original Markdown between the Markdown markers.
- Do not replace the Markdown marker content with status commentary such as 'already valid', 'no changes needed', or 'review complete'.
- The first non-frontmatter content inside the Markdown markers must be the top-level document title heading.
- The review report should list changed prose, source evidence, and remaining concerns.

Original Markdown draft:
---
title: Interactive View
sidebar_label: Interactive View
category: views-components
---

# Interactive View

InteractiveView is a View subclass with built-in interactive behavior for creating clickable UI components.

## Table of Contents

- [Creating an InteractiveView](#creating-an-interactiveview)
- [Configuring Click Behavior](#configuring-click-behavior)
- [Handling Click Events](#handling-click-events)
- [Responding to Press State Changes](#responding-to-press-state-changes)
- [Detecting Long Press Gestures](#detecting-long-press-gestures)
- [Disabling Interaction with Pseudo Disabled State](#disabling-interaction-with-pseudo-disabled-state)
- [Deriving Custom Interactive Components](#deriving-custom-interactive-components)

## Creating an InteractiveView

Create an `InteractiveView` using the static `New()` method. The view is clickable by default.

```cpp
using namespace Dali::Ui;

InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::New();
view.SetRequestedWidth(200.0f)
    .SetRequestedHeight(60.0f);
```

To downcast a base handle to an `InteractiveView`, use `DownCast()`:

```cpp
InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::DownCast(someHandle);
if (view)
{
  // Successfully downcast
}
```

## Configuring Click Behavior

### Enabling and Disabling Clicks

Control whether the view responds to clicks using `SetClickable()`. Query the current state with `IsClickable()`.

```cpp
InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::New();

view.SetClickable(false);  // Disable clicking
if (!view.IsClickable())
{
  // View will not respond to taps
}

view.SetClickable(true);   // Re-enable clicking
```

### Key Click Policy

For keyboard navigation, configure when a key press triggers a click using `SetKeyClickPolicy()`. Retrieve the current policy with `GetKeyClickPolicy()`.

```cpp
view.SetKeyClickPolicy(KeyClickPolicy::ON_PRESS);
KeyClickPolicy policy = view.GetKeyClickPolicy();
```

## Handling Click Events

Connect to the `ClickedSignal()` to respond when the user taps or clicks the view. The signal provides the view and the input event that triggered the click.

### Using ConnectClickedSignal for Fluent Chaining

The `ConnectClickedSignal()` method returns a reference to the view, enabling method chaining during setup:

```cpp
class MyController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void SetupView()
  {
    InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::New()
      .SetRequestedWidth(200.0f)
      .SetRequestedHeight(60.0f)
      .ConnectClickedSignal(this, &MyController::OnClicked);
  }

  void OnClicked(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    // Handle click
  }
};
```

### Direct Signal Connection

Alternatively, connect to `ClickedSignal()` directly:

```cpp
view.ClickedSignal().Connect(this, &MyController::OnClicked);
```

Multiple handlers can be connected to the same signal:

```cpp
view.ConnectClickedSignal(this, &MyController::OnClickedFirst);
view.ConnectClickedSignal(this, &MyController::OnClickedSecond);
```

## Responding to Press State Changes

Track when the user presses down or releases by connecting to `PressedChangedSignal()`. The callback receives the view, a boolean indicating the pressed state, and the input event.

```cpp
class ButtonController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void CreateButton()
  {
    mButton = InteractiveView::New()
      .SetRequestedWidth(150.0f)
      .SetRequestedHeight(50.0f)
      .ConnectPressedChangedSignal(this, &ButtonController::OnPressedChanged);
  }

  void OnPressedChanged(View view, bool pressed, InputEvent event)
  {
    if (pressed)
    {
      // User pressed down - show visual feedback
    }
    else
    {
      // User released - restore normal appearance
    }
  }

private:
  InteractiveView mButton;
};
```

Query the current pressed state at any time using `IsPressed()`:

```cpp
if (view.IsPressed())
{
  // View is currently in pressed state
}
```

## Detecting Long Press Gestures

Connect to `LongPressedSignal()` to detect when the user performs a long press gesture. The handler must return a boolean: return `true` to suppress the subsequent click event, or `false` to allow both the long press and click to fire.

```cpp
class LongPressController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void SetupView()
  {
    InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::New()
      .SetRequestedWidth(100.0f)
      .SetRequestedHeight(100.0f)
      .ConnectLongPressedSignal(this, &LongPressController::OnLongPressed);
  }

  bool OnLongPressed(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    // Handle long press
    return true;  // Suppress the click that would follow
  }
};
```

For direct signal connection:

```cpp
view.LongPressedSignal().Connect(this, &LongPressController::OnLongPressed);
```

## Disabling Interaction with Pseudo Disabled State

Use the pseudo disabled state to visually indicate that a view is non-interactive while keeping it in the view hierarchy. This is useful for showing disabled buttons or controls.

```cpp
InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::New();

view.SetPseudoDisabled(true);  // Mark as disabled
if (view.IsPseudoDisabled())
{
  // View is in pseudo disabled state
}

view.SetPseudoDisabled(false);  // Re-enable
```

Connect to `PseudoDisabledChangedSignal()` to react to state changes:

```cpp
view.PseudoDisabledChangedSignal().Connect(this, &MyController::OnPseudoDisabledChanged);

void OnPseudoDisabledChanged(View view, bool disabled)
{
  if (disabled)
  {
    // Update visual appearance to show disabled state
  }
}
```

## Deriving Custom Interactive Components

`InteractiveView` is designed as a base class for custom interactive components. Use the `DALI_UI_CHAIN_INTERACTIVEVIEW_METHODS` macro in your derived class to inherit all chaining methods.

```cpp
class MyButton : public InteractiveView
{
public:
  MyButton() = default;

  static MyButton New(const Dali::String& text)
  {
    MyButton button(InteractiveView::New());
    button.Initialize(text);
    return button;
  }

  static MyButton DownCast(BaseHandle handle)
  {
    InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::DownCast(handle);
    return view ? MyButton(view) : MyButton();
  }

  void Initialize(const Dali::String& text)
  {
    // Set up button appearance
  }

  // Inherit all chaining methods from InteractiveView
  DALI_UI_CHAIN_INTERACTIVEVIEW_METHODS(MyButton)

private:
  explicit MyButton(InteractiveView view)
  : InteractiveView(view)
  {
  }
};
```

The macro provides overridden `SetClickable()`, `SetPseudoDisabled()`, `SetKeyClickPolicy()`, and signal connection methods that return the derived type, preserving fluent chaining:

```cpp
MyButton button = MyButton::New("Click Me")
  .SetClickable(true)
  .SetPseudoDisabled(false)
  .ConnectClickedSignal(this, &MyController::OnButtonClicked);
